item.title: Shop Callback Phishing, SaaS Cookie Theft, and Windows 10 ESU Updates
Gaslight macOS Malware, Bluekit BITM Phishing, and Chrome Ad Blocker Injection Risks
DCloud Scam Infrastructure, curl CVE-2026-8932, Hoppscotch CVE-2026-50160, and Ukrposhta Cyberattack
Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke; Leo Platform npm Packages Compromised
Cisco SD-WAN CVE-2026-20245 Zero-Day Exploited; Netskope Warns on Shadow AI Risks
npm and GitHub Actions Supply Chain Attacks, Cisco CVE-2026-20245 Zero-Day Root Exploit, and Gaslight Malware AI Evasion
item.content:encoded:
Miasma Mini Shai-Hulud Hits LeoPlatform npm Packages and GitHub Actions, Expands to the Go Ecosystem — Must Read

A supply chain attack campaign tied to the Miasma family has compromised numerous npm packages and a Go module, using novel install-trigger techniques and targeting developer CI/CD environments, GitHub Actions, and AI coding assistants to steal credentials and propagate.

  • The latest Miasma wave compromised 23 npm packages and a Go module by adding a malicious binding.gyp file that triggers hidden JavaScript execution during installation.
  • The payload steals a broad set of credentials from developer machines and CI/CD environments, including npm/PyPI tokens, GitHub tokens, cloud keys, SSH keys, and Kubernetes configs.
  • Attackers heavily target GitHub Actions workflows to harvest secrets, upload stolen data, and plant malicious repository branches and workflows disguised as Dependabot activity.
  • Persistence hooks are embedded for AI coding assistants and IDEs (Claude, VS Code, Cursor, Gemini), causing the malware to execute when a poisoned repository is opened locally.
  • The campaign marker 'RevokeAndItGoesKaboom' directly ties this wave to earlier GitHub Actions compromises, such as the codfish/semantic-release-action incident, confirming shared attacker tradecraft.

Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access — Must Read

An unknown threat actor exploited the Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 as early as 2025 to gain root access on a communications service provider's device, months before public disclosure.

  • Mandiant discovered that CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager was exploited as a zero-day at least two months before public disclosure.
  • The initial compromise likely involved earlier zero-day authentication bypass flaws CVE-2026-20127 or CVE-2026-20182 to establish unauthorized peering connections.
  • After regaining access via stolen certificates from a prior breach, the attacker exploited CVE-2026-20245 by uploading a malicious CSV file (evil_tenant.csv) to create a hidden root account named 'troot'.
  • The threat actor used anti-forensic techniques, including deleting files, restoring original configurations, and resetting the admin password to its default to remain undetected.
  • The incident highlights a continuing trend of adversaries targeting edge devices like SD-WAN that lack endpoint detection, allowing persistent visibility into internal network traffic.

Bluekit phishing kit adopts browser-in-the-middle for login theft — Must Read

The Bluekit phishing kit now uses the legitimate rrweb library to perform browser-in-the-middle attacks, relaying victim inputs to an attacker-controlled browser to steal session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication.

  • Bluekit has shifted from adversary-in-the-middle to browser-in-the-middle (BitM) using the open-source rrweb library to relay victims’ DOM and keystrokes to an attacker-controlled browser.
  • The BitM technique allows attackers to steal valid session tokens and gain unlimited account access even when multi-factor authentication is in place.
  • Bluekit employs multiple anti-analysis measures, including randomized CSS filters, obfuscated JavaScript bundles, custom CAPTCHA, browser fingerprinting, and WebRTC IP mismatch detection.
  • Netcraft reported nearly 70 new Bluekit hostnames identified over a single week, indicating active and rapid deployment of the phishing kit.

New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns — Must Read

A new stealthy backdoor named Mistic, linked to the KongTuke initial access broker, has been deployed in opportunistic ClickFix campaigns since April 2026 to establish low-visibility persistence and has ties to Qilin ransomware deployment.

  • A stealthy backdoor named Mistic (MLTBackdoor) has been deployed in attacks since April 2026 targeting multiple sectors including insurance, education, IT, and professional services.
  • Mistic is linked to the initial access broker KongTuke (Woodgnat) and is delivered alongside ModeloRAT through ClickFix campaigns that trick users into running malicious commands.
  • The malware uses DLL side-loading via the trusted Microsoft binary MpExtMs.exe, runs payloads in memory, and can delete itself via a kill switch to avoid leaving forensic traces.
  • The threat actor operates a traffic distribution system on compromised WordPress sites and also uses fake Microsoft Teams messages from an IT Support account to distribute malware.
  • ModeloRAT has been observed in incidents that led to Qilin ransomware deployment, showing the group provides access to ransomware affiliates.

New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis — Should Read

A newly discovered North Korean macOS backdoor, Gaslight, uses prompt injection to trick AI analysts into aborting their review, while exfiltrating browser data and keychain contents via Telegram.

  • Gaslight is a Rust-based macOS backdoor and information stealer attributed with high confidence to a North Korean threat actor.
  • The malware embeds prompt injection payloads that feed fabricated system-failure messages into AI-assisted analysis tools, aiming to make them abort or refuse inspection.
  • It uses a Telegram bot API for command and control, supporting commands such as shell execution, process termination, and file exfiltration, and maintains persistence via a LaunchAgent.
  • An embedded Python stealer collects terminal history, installed applications, running processes, system profile, macOS Keychain, and browser data from Chrome, Brave, Firefox, and Safari, then exfiltrates it as a ZIP archive over Telegram.
  • The implant supplies its Telegram bot token at runtime rather than hardcoding it, and self-redacts that token from its own output to avoid exposure in logs or crash artifacts.

Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability — Should Read

A Chrome ad-blocking extension with over 10 million installs contains dormant, server-activated script injection capabilities that could execute arbitrary JavaScript on any website without user notice or a store review, though no malicious payload has been observed yet.

  • Adblock for YouTube, a Chrome extension with over 10 million installs, contains a dormant capability to execute arbitrary JavaScript on any website through a server-side configuration change without requiring an extension update or store review.
  • The extension's check to limit its function to YouTube only looks for the string 'youtube.com' anywhere in a URL, allowing it to be trivially bypassed on any site.
  • No malicious payload has been observed using this dormant script injection path, but three related extensions from the same owner were previously removed from the Chrome Web Store for malware.
  • Separately, Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 identified 18 browser extensions impersonating consumer brands to carry out affiliate marketing fraud.

Russia used Cellebrite phone-hacking tool to crack down on dissident after firm cut off country — Should Read

Despite Cellebrite cutting off Russia in March 2021, authorities used the company's legacy UFED tool to extract data from dissident Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone three months later, underscoring the firm's inability to disable existing hardware and prevent continued abuse by repressive regimes.

  • Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED to extract data from dissident Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone12 in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite cut off Russia over human rights concerns.
  • Forensic analysis of MobileLockdown records revealed USB connections to a device with a Host ID previously attributed to Cellebrite, confirming the tool's use.
  • Court records show that documents extracted from Pivovarov's phone using the UFED were used to build a case against him, leading to a four-year prison sentence in July 2022.
  • Cellebrite management stated that any use of legacy hardware in Russia after March 2021 was unauthorized, but Citizen Lab said the technology's architecture made it difficult to meaningfully cut off abusive customers.
  • Citizen Lab and Access Now sent a letter to Cellebrite urging the company to stop selling to regimes with histories of abuse and to conduct human rights due diligence.

Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks — Should Read

Threat actors are abusing Shopify's popular Shop order-tracking app to inject fake purchase receipts into users' histories, making callback phishing scams appear more legitimate and tricking victims into divulging sensitive information or installing remote access software.

  • Threat actors are inserting fake purchase receipts into the order history of the Shop app, a legitimate order-tracking app from Shopify.
  • The fake receipts impersonate brands like Norton, McAfee, Apple, and PayPal and display phone numbers that lead to scammers posing as support agents.
  • Scammers use these calls to trick victims into revealing account credentials, payment card details, authentication codes, or to install remote access software.
  • The abuse is a callback phishing technique that is more effective than email-based methods because users inherently trust the Shop app.
  • Users are advised not to call the numbers on suspicious receipts and to verify any alleged charges directly with their bank.

From San Pedro to Salinas: How a Chinese Framework “DCloud Uni-App” Powers a Global Scam Economy — Should Read

The Chinese DCloud Uni-App framework serves as the technical backbone for a massive, globally distributed scam economy encompassing over 236,000 domains, allowing centralized operators to rapidly build and deploy fraudulent investment platforms, pig-butchering schemes, and other scams; security teams can use its distinct code signatures to track and block this infrastructure.

  • The DCloud Uni-App framework has been used to build at least 236,493 distinct second-level domain scam sites since 2022, with a steep increase in new registrations after the RainbowEx scam gained international media attention.
  • The same investment scam template family has fueled physical-world fraud operations in the United States, including the Lightning Shared Scooter Co. (LSSC) scooter scam and the currently active Yuechi Sharing Technology Ltd. bicycle scam, which obtained a genuine U.S. Treasury FinCEN MSB registration to appear legitimate.
  • Technical fingerprints, hosting patterns, and coordinated domain registration dips suggest one or more large centralized operators control a significant share of the DCloud-built investment scam infrastructure, with more evasive operators favoring bulletproof providers such as CTG Server.
  • Enterprise exposure is widespread but diffuse: over 985 distinct enterprise customers' devices generated more than five million DNS queries to DCloud-built scam domains, indicating employees are encountering these scams on personal devices and corporate networks.
  • The reusable code fingerprints of the DCloud framework and additional scam-specific markers give defenders a reliable way to detect and block this fraud ecosystem at the DNS layer before users can reach the scam websites.

Inside the 2026 SMB threat landscape: From phishing and scams to fake AI tools — Should Read

In 2026, attackers are exploiting the AI hype by disguising malware as popular AI tools—attacks on SMBs using such lures surged nearly 5x—while SMBs remain the primary target for initial access sales on dark web forums, often as stepping stones to larger enterprises.

  • From January to April 2026, Kaspersky solutions detected 33,352 attacks on SMBs that used malware disguised as five popular AI tools, an almost fivefold increase from the same period in 2025.
  • Kaspersky blocked 414,736 attacks on SMB users where malicious software or potentially unwanted applications were disguised as popular communication apps, showing that fake messenger and video conferencing software remains a widespread threat.
  • More than half of dark web forum posts offering initial access to corporate infrastructures in early 2026 related to small and medium-sized businesses, likely because they are less protected and can serve as trusted third parties for larger organizations.
  • Phishing and scam campaigns in 2026 used fake AI services, fake compliance issues from companies like Apple, and fraudulent online document notifications to steal business credentials, personal data, and money.

Your Employees Logged In Without Issue; Attackers Logged In Too. — Should Read

Attackers are increasingly stealing authenticated session cookies to bypass MFA and silently access enterprise platforms like Workday and Snowflake, and organizations must add cookie monitoring to detect these exposures before they lead to breaches.

  • In January 2026, five malicious Chrome extensions were found stealing session cookies from Workday, NetSuite, and SAP SuccessFactors every 60 seconds and sending them to attacker servers.
  • A stolen session cookie lets an attacker bypass login and MFA entirely by importing it into a browser, gaining immediate authenticated access without triggering alert events.
  • Infostealer malware commonly harvests entire browser cookie stores, and infections on personal or unmanaged devices can capture corporate session tokens without IT visibility.
  • The Snowflake campaign of 2024-2025 used infostealer-harvested credentials and session tokens to compromise over 160 organizations, including Ticketmaster, Santander Bank, and AT&T.
  • MFA, credential monitoring, zero trust access controls, and EDR all fail to stop session cookie theft because they protect the authentication event rather than the session token that follows.

Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027 — Should Read

Microsoft has quietly extended the free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program for consumers by one year, now covering security updates until October 12, 2027, to give users more time to upgrade to Windows 11.

  • Microsoft quietly extended the free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates (ESU) program for consumers by an additional year, now available until October 12, 2027.
  • The extension was made without a formal announcement, appearing only in updates to Microsoft's ESU documentation and as an editor's note on a Windows Experience Blog post.
  • Consumers can receive the free ESU by paying $30, backing up their Windows settings to a Microsoft account, redeeming 1,000 Microsoft reward points, or, in the European Economic Area, by logging in to Windows 10 with a Microsoft account.
  • The consumer ESU program is only for personal devices and is not available for systems joined to Active Directory domains, Microsoft Entra, or managed through Mobile Device Management (though Microsoft Entra-registered devices are eligible).
  • Users already enrolled in the ESU program will automatically remain covered until the new October 2027 end date, and a single ESU license can be used on up to ten devices associated with the same Microsoft account.

Ukraine's state postal operator reports app disruption after cyberattack — Should Read

Ukraine's state postal operator Ukrposhta suffered a mobile app outage following a cyberattack claimed by the pro-Russian hacktivist group IT Army of Russia, which alleges it exfiltrated user data, though this remains unverified.

  • Ukraine’s state postal operator Ukrposhta reported that its mobile app experienced temporary disruption following an overnight cyberattack on its IT systems.
  • The pro-Russian hacktivist group IT Army of Russia claimed responsibility and alleged it had exfiltrated a database of user information, though this claim has not been independently verified.
  • Ukrposhta did not disclose the attacker’s identity or confirm whether any data was compromised, and stated no other services were affected beyond the app.
  • The IT Army of Russia emerged in March 2025 and uses cybercrime forums and Telegram to publish stolen data, recruit insiders, and solicit intelligence on Ukrainian infrastructure.
  • Ukrposhta had previously experienced a cyber incident in 2024 that disrupted payment processing and parcel deliveries through an attack on a partner’s IT infrastructure.

PirloTV sports piracy network disrupted as 44 domains seized — May Read

Authorities seized 44 domains linked to the PirloTV sports piracy network, which generated over 950 million annual visits, in a joint operation by ACE, UEFA, UC3, and Mexican agencies ahead of major soccer events.

  • A coalition including ACE, UEFA, UC3, and Mexican authorities seized 44 domains linked to the PirloTV sports piracy network.
  • The targeted domains collectively generated more than 950 million visits per year, with approximately 230 million of those coming from Mexico.
  • The domain seizures occurred ahead of the UEFA Champions League final on May 30 and during the ongoing FIFA World Cup, potentially affecting illegal streaming access across Latin America.
  • Despite the takedown, PirloTV is known for quickly migrating to new domains, and some domains still indexed at the time of writing continued to offer live sports streams from broadcasters like ESPN and Fox Sports.
  • The action marks the first collaboration between ACE and Mexico's Institute of Industrial Property under a newly signed anti-piracy Memorandum of Understanding.

Another Russian dairy company reportedly disrupted by cyberattack — May Read

A cyberattack on Russian dairy producer Ufagormolzavod disrupted logistics and accounting, forcing manual operations, and adds to a growing series of cyber incidents targeting the country's dairy sector.

  • A cyberattack on dairy producer Ufagormolzavod in Russia's Bashkortostan region disrupted logistics and accounting, forcing manual processing of shipments, though production continued.
  • The company did not disclose the attackers, whether data was compromised, or when IT systems would be restored.
  • Earlier the same week, the website of state-owned dairy provider Molochnaya Kukhnya was compromised and false claims of power outages and bacterial contamination were posted.
  • The incident adds to a series of cyberattacks on Russia's dairy sector, including a past disruption of the veterinary certification system and ransomware attacks on Semyonishna and Kabosh.

A Compromised Vendor Account Sent an ACH Redirect With a Fabricated Closure Deadline and Mismatched Phone Numbers — May Read

An attacker used a compromised 26-year-old vendor account to send an ACH banking-change request with a fabricated same-day deadline; the tell was two signature blocks with phone numbers differing by one digit.

  • An attacker used a compromised vendor email account from a 26-year-old domain to request an ACH banking change from a construction firm's accounts payable contact.
  • The email fabricated a same-day account closure deadline (March 31, 2026) to create urgency, with no invoice or prior billing context.
  • The message contained two signature blocks for the same person with phone numbers differing by one digit, indicating at least one was fabricated.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI detected the message at 68% confidence based on behavioral signals without any malicious link.
  • The primary defense recommended is an out-of-band callback to the vendor using a known number sourced outside the email.

DHS chief says president has met with potential CISA nominee; agency plans to hire 600 — May Read

DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin said the president has met with a potential CISA director and the agency plans to hire 600 people to rebuild after workforce cuts, while also expressing concern about cybersecurity threats from China.

  • Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin testified that the president has met with a potential CISA director, with Palantir CTO Shyam Sankar considered the likely nominee.
  • Mullin said CISA needs about 600 new hires after workforce cuts and that rebuilding the agency will probably take a year once a director is confirmed.
  • Mullin highlighted daily efforts by China to breach U.S. systems and said CISA will be crucial in defending them, noting that private companies cannot do it alone.
  • Mullin requested new clarity from Congress on the limits of CISA’s authority in cybersecurity.

Introduction to COM usage by Windows threats — May Read

Malware often leverages Windows COM interfaces for persistence, lateral movement, and evasion, requiring analysts to map CLSIDs, IIDs, and vtable calls to understand hidden functionality.

  • Windows COM technology enables communication between programs in different languages and is abused by threat actors for lateral movement, execution, persistence, and evasion.
  • Malware families such as Gh0stRAT, WarmCookie, Attor, and Qakbot leverage COM interfaces to interact with Task Scheduler, BITS, and WMI, avoiding detection by using indirect vtable calls instead of spawning command-line tools.
  • Reverse engineers can recover meaningful class, interface, and method names from opaque GUIDs and indirect calls by using tools like OleView.NET, ComView, and IDA Pro's COM Helper.
  • Analysts can hunt for COM usage by searching for ProgID strings or little-endian byte patterns of CLSIDs and IIDs in binaries, then filter results with context to distinguish malware from legitimate software.
  • DCOM extends COM across the network, allowing remote object activation over RPC for lateral movement, recognized as MITRE ATT&CK technique T1021.003.

Webinar: Why account takeovers remain one of the hardest threats to stop — Optional

Account takeover attacks remain hard to detect because they blend in with normal business activity using legitimate credentials, but behavioral AI can automate detection and response to help security teams limit the damage faster.

  • Account takeover attacks often rely on legitimate credentials, trusted devices, and normal business communications, making them difficult to distinguish from normal user activity.
  • Because attackers can blend into normal business operations, traditional security controls often struggle to detect account compromise quickly.
  • Behavioral AI can automate the detection of unusual behavior patterns, investigation, and remediation workflows, helping security teams respond faster.
  • The webinar will examine how phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks lead to compromised business accounts and present practical techniques for reducing response times.

AI Turned The Patch Window Into The Threat Window — Optional

AI-driven vulnerability discovery is outpacing remediation, so organizations must shift from trying to patch everything to enforcing data-centric security and zero-trust access controls that limit exposure while patches are developed.

  • Anthropic's Project Glasswing surfaced 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across critical enterprise software in a single month, highlighting that vulnerability discovery is far outpacing remediation.
  • The median time to remediate a high-severity vulnerability is two weeks, making it impossible to patch everything as AI-driven disclosures accelerate.
  • Attackers also have access to advanced AI models, shrinking the patch window and turning it into a race that defenders cannot win by patching alone.
  • CVE-2025-32711, a zero-click prompt injection in Microsoft 365 Copilot, demonstrates how AI-enabled attacks can exfiltrate data silently, bypassing traditional perimeter and signature-based defenses.
  • 98% of organizations report unmanaged AI use, and only 12% can detect all shadow AI activity, according to a Netskope report.

Cloud Security Incident Response: 7 Best Practices | Mitiga — Optional

Effective cloud incident response hinges on centralizing cloud-native control-plane and identity logs before they expire, adopting least privilege and zero trust to limit blast radius, and proactively testing response plans that account for shared responsibility boundaries where traditional disk forensics don't apply.

  • Cloud incident response depends on cloud-native telemetry such as control-plane logs, identity events, and API activity because there is often no endpoint to image.
  • Under the shared responsibility model, the cloud provider is responsible for security 'of' the cloud, while the customer is responsible for security 'in' the cloud, including their information and identities.
  • Logs are critical for cloud environments, and organizations should configure, centralize, and securely retain them for a sufficient period—likely one year or longer—to ensure they are available during an investigation.
  • Applying the principle of least privilege and zero trust limits the damage if credentials are compromised by giving users and resources only the access they need and continuously verifying every transaction.
  • Preserving evidence in a cloud incident may require storing EC2 instance metadata, EBS disk snapshots, and various logs, rather than creating traditional forensic disk images.

Rural Hospitals Turn to Threat Intelligence Networks to Strengthen Cybersecurity Defenses — Optional

Resource-constrained rural hospitals can meaningfully improve their cybersecurity by joining collaborative threat intelligence networks and adopting basic cyber hygiene practices, rather than relying solely on in-house defenses they cannot afford.

  • More than 2,000 rural hospitals in the United States are chronically underfunded and understaffed, leading cybersecurity to be deprioritized and exposing them to cyberattacks.
  • Cybersecurity experts and threat intelligence initiatives tend to focus on high-value health-sector targets like major hospital networks and insurance companies, overlooking rural hospitals.
  • Limited cybersecurity resources mean fewer defenses, making rural hospitals attractive targets for ransomware and phishing attacks.
  • Rural hospitals are increasingly leveraging collaborative threat intelligence networks and basic cyber hygiene practices to close security gaps and better protect patient data.

The Parable of the Bullet Holes: AI Security Edition — Optional

Organizations suffer from survivorship bias by only monitoring sanctioned AI systems for minor incidents, while catastrophic data leaks happen unseen in public LLMs and shadow AI that are never inspected.

  • During World War II, statistician Abraham Wald revealed survivorship bias by showing that bullet holes on returning planes only indicated non-critical hit areas, while catastrophic damage to engines and cockpits left no survivors in the data.
  • Companies monitoring only their approved enterprise LLMs for minor policy violations repeat this bias, treating visible 'bullet holes' as all risks while ignoring the fact that fatal breaches occur outside the monitored systems.
  • The most dangerous missing data includes employees inadvertently pasting customer PII into public LLMs and development teams fine-tuning unauthorized AI instances with proprietary source code, neither of which appears in enterprise audit logs.
  • Survivorship bias makes organizations optimize for survival in their sanctioned systems rather than preventing the catastrophic, invisible breaches from shadow AI and unmonitored services.
  • The article calls for security teams to become like Abraham Wald and actively hunt for critical gaps in what is not being monitored, such as personal accounts and embedded AI in SaaS.

Health-ISAC AAMI eXhange Interview – Medical Device Security — Optional

Health-ISAC’s Medical Device Security Analyst Taylor Porter discussed the latest threats facing medical devices during an interview at the AAMI eXchange conference.

  • Health-ISAC's Medical Device Security Analyst Taylor Porter participated in an interview at the AAMI eXchange conference on June 25, 2026.
  • During the interview with AAMI Connect, Porter discussed the latest threats affecting medical device security.
  • AAMI Connect hosts Danielle McGeary and Robert Burroughs were pictured alongside the interview coverage.
  • The article provides links to related resources on topics such as rural hospital cybersecurity, AI adoption in healthcare, and China-nexus threat groups.

ThreatsDay Bulletin: Smart TV Proxyware, 24-Year curl Bug, AI Crime Forums + 13 More Stories — Optional

This week's threats underscore that attackers don't need advanced techniques—forgotten credentials, hidden proxyware in smart TV apps, fake updates, and phishing through collaboration tools are still highly effective for compromise.

  • Over one-third of LG and Samsung smart TV apps reviewed by Spur Intelligence contain hidden proxyware that can relay third-party traffic through the TV owner's internet connection without meaningful owner awareness or consent.
  • AISLE discovered six security flaws in curl, including CVE-2026-8932, the oldest reported curl vulnerability, which has been present in releases since March 2001.
  • A maximum-severity flaw (CVE-2026-50160) in self-hosted Hoppscotch allows an unauthenticated attacker to overwrite the JWT signing key via a single HTTP request, leading to complete server compromise.
  • Klue revealed that a stolen credential from a 2022 pilot was used by the Icarus extortionists to breach Salesforce data from numerous corporate customers, including multiple cybersecurity companies.
  • NCC Group reports that nation-state actors are increasingly adopting cybercriminal tactics and sharing infrastructure with ransomware groups to disguise espionage operations.

Breach At The Beach: The Ultimate Entra ID Training Experience — Optional

Varonis Threat Labs launched Breach at the Beach, a free online CTF that trains defenders to detect real-world Entra ID attack techniques, offering CPE credits and in-person prizes at Black Hat USA and DEF CON.

  • Varonis Threat Labs researchers Doron Kapah and Mark Vaitsman created Breach at the Beach, a free online CTF that lets security practitioners practice detecting real-world Entra ID attack techniques through hands-on challenges.
  • The CTF is designed to teach players how threats abuse legitimate Entra ID features, not misconfigurations, and was intentionally built to prevent AI assistants from solving the challenges so that participants genuinely absorb the lessons.
  • Completing all four stages of the CTF awards participants CPE credits, a certificate of completion, and entry into a drawing for a $2,000 gift card if finished by August 6 during Black Hat USA.
  • The exercise is grounded in actual attacks investigated by Varonis researchers, covering modern concerns like non-human identities and AI-related visibility gaps that many defenders miss in their daily work.
  • The researchers will present and assist players in person at Black Hat USA 2026 and DEF CON 34’s Cloud Village, where top DEF CON participants can also compete for prizes.

The Four Elevations of Effective Fraud Prevention — Optional

Effective fraud prevention requires monitoring at transaction, account, platform, and network levels to detect sophisticated schemes like account takeovers early, using behavioral signals and shared intelligence.

  • Effective fraud prevention requires monitoring every customer touchpoint, from account creation to checkout and customer service interactions.
  • Four elevations are necessary: transaction, account, platform, and network levels, each providing deeper context to detect advanced fraud methods.
  • Account-level monitoring contrasts a fraudster's behavior against historical trusted activity, quickly revealing suspicious actions like changing contact info or ordering additional cards.
  • Platform-level monitoring groups accounts to identify fraud rings and multi-account attacks using shared geolocation, device intelligence, and IP resolution.
  • Network-level partnerships allow organizations to act on known fraud indicators (e.g., phone numbers, devices, addresses) reported by peers, stopping attacks immediately.

Surviving the Mythos Era: Richard Bejtlich on the Case for NDR — Optional

Security teams should adopt network detection and response (NDR) with hypothesis-driven threat hunting to improve interdiction and gather defensible evidence during investigations.

  • Alerts are becoming less useful as vulnerability discovery accelerates, so security teams need validated evidence of active exploit and exposure rather than just more raw telemetry.
  • Resilient security programs should focus on interdiction — identifying and disrupting malicious activity before attackers achieve their objectives.
  • Threat hunting must start with a hypothesis about adversarial techniques, then analysts query network logs and sessions to validate or disprove that theory.
  • AI can assist network detection and response by optimizing alert frameworks, enabling agentic triage, and coordinating tools, but human verification remains a critical control point.
  • A 'zero-baseline' strategy for initial alert rules prevents alert fatigue, and every alert should be treated as the start of an investigation, not a conclusive definition of an event.

item.title: simonecorsi/mawesome GitHub Action Compromise and Google Privacy Controls Rollout
